title: Unit 16 Hyper-Exhaustive Masterclass: The Imperative Mood (Giving Orders)

script.text: Welcome... to the Hyper-Exhaustive Grammar Masterclass for Unit 16: The Imperative Mood. This is 'Boss Mode'. To give an order... a piece of advice... or simple instructions... you need this mood. Since you can only give an order directly to the person you are talking to... there are only two forms: 'You' singular and 'You' plural. I'm William... and here with me is Athina. Athina... how does a Greek person decide how to give an order?

Γεια σας! Just like everything else in Greek... it’s all about the lens. Do you want someone to do something once... a 'Photo'? Or keep doing it as a habit... a 'Video'? We have two different imperatives for that. But let's be honest... 99% of the time... we use the 'Photo' one.
The Simple Imperative is the king. To build it... we use the Simple Stem we learned in Unit 12. Athina... give us the pattern for the verb 'to write'... 'Ghrá-pho'.
Simple Stem is 'Ghráp-'. For one person... add 'E'. 'Ghráp-se!'... Write! For a group... add 'TE'. 'Ghráp-ste!'... Write, you all! It’s short... sharp... and direct.
Sharp and direct. And we have a native secret for the singular form. Vowel Dropping. What happens when we add 'it' or 'them' to the command?
Greeks love speed! 'Ghráp-se to'... sounds slow. Usually... we drop the 'E' and merge them. 'Ghráps' to!'... Write it! 'Phé-re ta' becomes 'Phér' ta!'... Bring them! It sounds much more natural.
Much faster. Now... what about Continuous Commands? 'Keep doing it!'. Using the Present Stem.

We use these for general rules or habits. 'Tré-kho'... to run. 'Tré-khe!'... Keep running! 'Thi-a-vá-zo'... to read. 'Thee-á-va-ze!'... Keep reading! Notice the stress... it jumps back to the third syllable from the end to keep the rhythm.
It jumps back for balance. Now... the 'Big 6' irregulars. These are the commands you will hear every single day in Greece. Athina... let’s list them for the singular.

Memorize these immediately: 'Pes!'... say. 'Des!'... look. 'Vres!'... find. 'Pyes!'... drink. 'É-la!'... come. And 'Peée-ghai-ne!'... go. They are tiny but they do all the heavy lifting in conversation.
Tiny but mighty. Now... how do we say 'DON'T'? This is a common trap.

You never use the Imperative for 'Don't'. Instead... you use the magic word 'Meen' and the Subjunctive form. 'Meen kleée-seis teen pór-ta!'... Don't close the door. It’s softer and more correct. If you just shout 'MEEN!'... that also works in an emergency!
Emergency 'Meen'. Final masterclass secret: the 'Yia' particle. How do we sound extra friendly while giving an order?
Just put 'Yia' right in front. 'Yia kát-se'... Go on and sit down. 'Yia é-la e-thó'... Come here for a second. It sounds much more like a suggestion than a command. It’s very inviting.

The secret to polite orders. You have now completed the exhaustive Masterclass for Unit 16. You can now give orders... keep routines... handle irregulars... and do it all with native politeness. Review the directions and commands vocabulary for more practice. Excellent work... we’ll see you in Unit 17: Adverbs and Expressions of Manner.
Πολύ καλά! Τα λέμε στο επόμενο μάθημα.
